steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The student claims opening weekend earnings can reliably predict Oscar recognition. To weaken that claim, we need a case where higher opening weekend earnings fail to produce Oscar nomination while lower earnings do.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• "*The Girl Who Played with Fire* was not recognized by the Oscars but had much higher opening weekend earnings than *Crouching Tiger, Hidden Dragon*, which was recognized by the Oscars.": The Girl Who Played with Fire had higher opening weekend earnings (about $905,000) than Crouching Tiger, Hidden Dragon (about $663,000), yet was not nominated while Crouching Tiger was. This directly contradicts the prediction. ✓
  • "Although both *Crouching Tiger, Hidden Dragon* and *Eat Drink Man Woman* were recognized by the Oscars, *Crouching Tiger, Hidden Dragon* had higher lifetime earnings.": compares total earnings, not opening weekend earnings, so it does not address the student's claim. ✗
  • "*Iron Monkey* had higher lifetime earnings than *Eat Drink Man Woman* did, even though *Eat Drink Man Woman* was recognized by the Oscars.": this compares lifetime earnings, not opening weekend earnings. ✗
  • "Although both *Iron Monkey* and *The Girl Who Played with Fire* were directed by well-known figures, neither film was recognized by the Oscars.": addresses a metric unrelated to opening weekend performance. ✗
That matches the correct answer — A is correct.
